record.field: authentik is a self-hosted, open-source identity provider that delivers single sign-on, multi-factor authentication and access control across every modern protocol.

authentik is a self-hosted, open-source identity provider that delivers single sign-on, multi-factor authentication and access control across every modern protocol.

It acts as the central authentication layer for your applications, supporting SAML 2.0, OAuth2/OIDC, LDAP, RADIUS and SCIM, plus a reverse-proxy outpost for apps that lack native SSO. A flexible flow-and-policy engine lets you model login, enrollment and MFA journeys, while blueprints let you version your entire identity configuration as YAML in Git. It is commonly deployed as a fully self-hosted alternative to Okta, Auth0, Entra ID and Ping Identity.

Key features
  • SAML 2.0, OAuth2/OIDC, LDAP, RADIUS and SCIM provider support from a single platform
  • Multi-factor and passwordless authentication with TOTP, WebAuthn and FIDO2 passkeys
  • Customizable flow engine and granular policies based on user attributes, time, location and risk
  • Forward-auth / reverse-proxy outpost to add SSO to applications without native support
  • Federation with external OAuth/SAML sources, social logins and directory sync (Entra ID, Google Workspace)
  • Roles, groups with multi-parent inheritance, self-service portal and YAML blueprints for GitOps
Best for

Teams and organizations that want full control over authentication and identity on their own infrastructure, consolidating SSO, MFA and user provisioning for internal tools and customer-facing apps without relying on a cloud identity vendor.

services: postgresql: image: docker.io/library/postgres:16-alpine restart: unless-stopped healthcheck: test: - CMD-SHELL - pg_isready -d $${POSTGRES_DB} -U $${POSTGRES_USER} start_period: 20s interval: 30s retries: 5 timeout: 5s volumes: - ./database:/var/lib/postgresql/data environment: POSTGRES_PASSWORD: {{ pg_pass }} POSTGRES_USER: {{ pg_user }} POSTGRES_DB: {{ pg_db }} expose: - 5432 container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_postgresql' networks: - internal redis: image: docker.io/library/redis:alpine command: --save 60 1 --loglevel warning restart: unless-stopped healthcheck: test: - CMD-SHELL - redis-cli ping | grep PONG start_period: 20s interval: 30s retries: 5 timeout: 3s volumes: - ./redis:/data expose: - 6379 container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_redis' networks: - internal server: image: ${AUTHENTIK_IMAGE:-ghcr.io/goauthentik/server}:${AUTHENTIK_TAG:-2025.6.3} restart: unless-stopped command: server environment: AUTHENTIK_SECRET_KEY: {{ authentik_secret_key }} AUTHENTIK_REDIS__HOST: redis AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__HOST: postgresql AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER: {{ pg_user }} AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__NAME: {{ pg_db }} AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__PASSWORD: {{ pg_pass }} volumes: - ./media:/media - ./custom_templates:/templates expose: - 9000 - 9443 depends_on: postgresql: condition: service_healthy redis: condition: service_healthy container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}' ports: - '{{ PORT_HTTP }}:9000' networks: - internal - agz-proxy-network worker: image: ${AUTHENTIK_IMAGE:-ghcr.io/goauthentik/server}:${AUTHENTIK_TAG:-2025.6.3} restart: unless-stopped command: worker environment: AUTHENTIK_SECRET_KEY: {{ authentik_secret_key }} AUTHENTIK_REDIS__HOST: redis AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__HOST: postgresql AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__USER: {{ pg_user }} AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__NAME: {{ pg_db }} AUTHENTIK_POSTGRESQL__PASSWORD: {{ pg_pass }} user: root volumes: - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock - ./media:/media - ./certs:/certs - ./custom_templates:/templates depends_on: postgresql: condition: service_healthy redis: condition: service_healthy container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_worker' networks: - internal networks: internal: driver: bridge agz-proxy-network: external: true

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Dump consistente de PostgreSQL (servicio en marcha) → $BACKUP_DATA_DIR. # Si POSTGRES_USER/POSTGRES_DB vienen vacíos (la imagen usa 'postgres' por defecto), # caemos a 'postgres': con $POSTGRES_USER vacío pg_dump intentaría el rol "root" del SO # y fallaría con «role "root" does not exist». docker compose exec -T postgresql sh -c 'pg_dump -U "${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}" -d "${POSTGRES_DB:-${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}}" --no-owner -Fc' \ > "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump" echo "pg_dump OK ($(wc -c < "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump") bytes)"

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Levanta solo la DB, espera readiness y restaura (mismo default a 'postgres') docker compose up -d postgresql for i in $(seq 1 30); do docker compose exec -T postgresql sh -c 'pg_isready -U "${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}"' && break sleep 2 done docker compose exec -T postgresql sh -c 'pg_restore -U "${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}" -d "${POSTGRES_DB:-${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres}}" --clean --if-exists --no-owner' \ < "$RESTORE_DATA_DIR/db.dump" # Verificación: la DB no quedó vacía (sh -c con dobles comillas; $VAR del contenedor con \$) N=$(docker compose exec -T postgresql sh -c "psql -U \${POSTGRES_USER:-postgres} -d \${POSTGRES_DB:-postgres} -tAc \"SELECT count(*) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public'\"") echo "tablas restauradas: $N" test "$N" -gt 0
